SIXPENCE
On the very threshold of this subject, I dash it to
pieces, and scatter it to the winds, as a piece of vile
and mischievous superstition; by saying, that man has
no such knowledge, neither are there any means by
which he can obtain such knowledge; and to the proof
of this assumption I proceed.
I have lately seen the subject of assumed astrological
influence, on the formation of the human character,
presumptuously dignified with the title of Zodiacal
Physiognomy. It will not pass with me, even under
that title. The author, of the name of Varley, prefaces
his subject with the unfounded assumption, that the
knowledge of planetary influence on human birth is
as certain as that the moon produces the tides of the
ocean. Not even so, as to the regularity of coincident
appearances; but if it were as regular in appearance,
and as certain, that amount of certainty is just nothing
at all; for it is by no means certain that the moon has
the least influence on the tides of the ocean.
It is an
assumption beginning to be much and irrefutably dis-
puted, inasmuch as the assumed principle of influence
is contrary to all mechanical action, and, if adopted,
accounts but for a small portion of the motions of the
Tides are only perceptible on the coasts. The
influx upon one must be accompanied by the reflux

her series of coasts; and it is much more likely
that such a change of influx and reflux is connected
more immediately with the motions of the earth itself,
than with the motions of the moon. The assumed
power of the moon to raise the tides an inch would be
a power equal to draw the whole ocean from its bed,
and from the earth. The reason why the moon has
been connected with the tides has been, because there
are uniform monthly appearances of each; but both
evidently derived from a common source, the motions
of the earth. So Mr. Varley's comparison is a failure
and it can be more easily shown that his principle is
a failure.

The consideration of this subject must be brought
under four general heads, because there are four classes
of pretension to explain it. In the order of seniority
they will stand thus: the Astrologer the Physiologist
-the Educationist-the Phrenologist.
The subject of the formation of character has evi-
dently engaged the attention of mankind from the
most remote periods, and is found mixed up with
every religion,with every species of moral instruc-
tion; with every secret association and pretension
to mystery, and with that which has been errone-
ously called occult science, which may be taken
to include religion and mystery; and, however defec-
tive in its principles, may, for the sake of illustration,
pass under the name of Astrology, or a reading or
learning of the stars, a discourse about starry influences.

ind to surrounding circumstances,
both physical and moral, that produce character.
There are also atmospherical and local influences,
which enter into the composition of man; such as that
of crowded cities, of the sea-coast, of the neighbour-
hood of large rivers, mines, romantic scenery, eleva-
tions, heath, forest, and morass; but it is man's mad
and vagrant conceit that can, alone induce him to look
beyond the earth's atmosphere, to explain anything in
the productions of the earth.
4
This comes of stupifying the brain with conceits
about the stars, and keeping the human race in a state
of perpetual childhood, by giving it a religion whereon
to waste its time, its means, and all its energies. It
is like giving it a riddle through life, which is not even
solved at death.
A volume of facts may be written against the claims
of those who seek an astral formation of character. In
an hour's discourse, a quarter of which can only be de-
inter-voted to this part of the subject, nothing more than
some general points, sufficient for elucidation, can be
introduced.
This, I think, has been done; the summary of which
is, that the evidence of variety, under the same astral
relations, both as to genus and nationality, and lo-
cality; the visible influence of family physiognomy,
the effects of education upon organization, to be ex
plained under the third head of inquiry, the utter im-
possibility of proving anything by unknown causes,
the proof that one failure falsifies the whole assump

ing about useful possessions, a low state of animal
life. It is a state of life, in which there is wanting the
mainspring to virtue, and to every degree of eminence
and happiness. Man may remain in such a state for
ages unknown, as was the case with some of the in-
four-habitants of the southern pacific ocean, and more par-
ticularly those of New Holland. Even in Europe,
with all the advantages of education and example, we
see men pass through life without receiving the stimu
lus to improve mind or bodily condition. We see
others receiving that stimulus, at different periods of
life; but never too late to be useful. If taken young,
distinction is sure to follow. If this be a correct view
of the human character in society, I claim the admis-
sion, that the course which we are now taking, in
free inquiry and free discussion,, is better calculated to
germinate and foster the stimulus to be desired, than
any religious ceremony that has ever been practised.
However melodious may be the church ceremony
however delightfully the organ may be played; how
ever sweet the voices that may sing; however solemn
the chaunt; and however impressive the religious dis-
course; it all passes and ends without intellectual im-
provement. It is all a pantomime to the intellect.
It is all an acting in parts; there is no sentiment,
reason, no intellectual sincerity, no conviction of
right, no comparative usefulness, no defence to be
made against criticism. The law will not allow it to
be laughed at, a

"Isis has been represented as the moon; Osiris as
the sun. She has been represented as a female bearing
a globe in one hand, and a bunch of corn in the other.
This is the most antient figure, and inscriptions under
her have been to this import: "I am all that has been,
that shall be, and none among mortals has hitherto
taken off my veil." Alluding to the mysteries of na-
ture. See the front of the Egyptian Hall, Piccadilly;
Our London Isis intends to take off her veil and
display nature to us in all its loveliness.
66

world? From you our Thames issues into existence.*

At the 20th verse of the eighteenth chapter of the
Gospel according to Saint Matthew, from which I
take the first part of my text, there are these words:
Where two or three are gathered together in my
name, there am I in the midst of them:" than which
nothing can be more plain:-Where two or three per-
sons are gathered together in pursuit of knowledge,
knowledge they will find among them.
The Gospel is the most ancient piece of writing in
existence that has taught us that knowledge is power,
and that it will go on to equalize mankind. We are
told that faith will remove mountains; but, for faith,
which, in our present state of language, is a word
without meaning, we must read knowledge; and we
know that, by the aid of mechanical power, we can
remove mountains, and cast them into the sea; but
we cannot do so, without the necessary knowledge of
mechanical power; nor can it be done by any other
means.
At the 32d verse of the twenty-second chapter, we
read :-" God is not the God of the dead, but of the
living;" which is so striking a truth, that it should
confound the advocates of life after death, while it
accords with my interpretation, that, as God is know-
ledge, God is the God of the living; for knowledge
has no concern with the dead, nor the dead with know-

M. ARAGO ON COMETS.
•
(Continued from The Times of March 16.)
Here the Moon will serve as a point of comparison.
The moon causes the tides of the ocean: mathematically speak-
ing, the comet of 1811 ought to have produced analagous tides,
but, as no one observed any such tides, we must admit that they
did not amount to an appreciable quantity. But the height of a
tide is proportioned to the attractive power. We know that the
lunar tides are very great, and that the cometary tides (if any) are
insensible; hence, it is clear that the cometary action on the
earth bears no proportion to that of the moon; and as to any
calorific effects of the great comet of 1811, the most delicate in-
struments have not shown their existence. Can, then, the wine-
growers expect any results from the comet of 1832 ?
11. ON THE PHYSICAL CONSTRUCTION OF COMETS-THAT IS,
THEIR NEBULOSITY, NUCLEUS, AND TAIL.
I have before spoken of the nebulosity, or hair, of the nucleus,
and of the tail of a comet. I will now detail all that telescopes
have enabled us to discover of their parts.
Many comets have no visible tail, many have been seen without
any visible nucleus; but in no case have comets been see

Pierre Apian as-
certained that the comet of 1531 carried its tail in every part of
its course, so as to have it always in a prolongation of the line
which joined the sun and the nucleus; but this principle has
been adopted too generally. It is true that for the most part the
tail is placed behind the comet, so as to stream out in a direction
opposed to the sun; but the line which joins those two luminaries
hardly ever corresponds with the axis of the til. Sometimes the
want of coincidence of these two lines is very great; indeed, in
some cases, the tail stands out at right angles from the line of
conjunction. In fact, it has been ascertained that the tail has
constantly an inclination towards the region which the comet has
left, as if, in its motion through a gaseous medium, the matter of
which the tail was formed had been more powerfully acted upon,
or resisted, than was experienced by the nucleus.
The tails become much larger, that is, wider, as they lengthen
out from the comet. In the middle of them is seen a dark band,
which divides them into two distinct, and often equal parts. An-
cient observers considered this band as the shadow of the body of
the comet. This explanation cannot be applied to the tails which
point towards the sun; perhaps we shall suit our hypothesis to
general appearances, if we suppose the tail to be a hollow cone,
which from well-known physical causes might be supposed to
present to us two exterior lines of light, separated by a space

text:
osed advantage
of being able to utter a paucity of ideas in two or more
languages. Words are but the signs of things, a token
in sounds; what we want is, the greatest possible amount
of knowledge of things. That is our God, a know-
ledge of the Lord; and we should strive to hasten the
time when it shall cover the earth, as the waters fill
the sea.
In what nobler light can I place the idea of God
before you, than that which is man's highest concep-
tion, the fountain of knowledge? Imagine the. Cre-
ator of all things, and you cannot imagine anything
higher than the power that is conscious of its own and
of all existence; the power that can compare and im-
prove, by arrangement, that can embellish nature by
art, and plan the means of its own happiness and im-
provement. Imagine the vastness, the apparent infi-
nity of the planetary systems, and all, in wonder, does
not equal animal life in its highest degrees. Man,
brief as is his span of life, is the God of the moral
world, and has everything to do for himself. Con-
cerning him, there is no other God; and that is the
sentiment of my text:—God is a spirit, and they that
worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth.
Truth is the principle of man's knowledge; worship
is the principle of his gratitude; and spirit the
principle of his nature; which makes up God and
the worship of God, the spirit and the truth. God is
the spirit of truth and our love of truth, and la-
bour to acquire knowledge, is our proper worship of
God.

The loves as well as the wars of the planets were
sung by the ancient poets; but whether they knew
more upon the subject than we now know is a ques-

Knowledge gives to man the advantage of recording
his life, and thus a remembrance of it in others is pro-
longed; but this adds nothing to the length of indi-
vidual life. The beauty and fragrance of a rose pre-
serves to us a remembrance of the flower; but the
flower itself survives not its month, without some arti-
ficial aid. The best of men is but the flower of a few
years; but lo, he droopeth and dieth, his beauty pass-
eth away; and wonderful must he have been, to sur-
vive the memory of a century.

form
varieties, under all his multiplied appellations-each
has the right, all others possess the wrong. And where,
among these contradictory and confounding faiths, is
one whose ipse dixit truths are compounded of facts;
whose first premises are demonstrable to the human
eye, the human ear, the human touch; whose proofs
are sought and found in the nature of man and the na-
ture of things, and whose conclusions are sanctioned
by our own confirming sensations and assenting
reason?
No, my friends! we have seen that no religion
stands on the basis of things known; none bounds its
horizon within the field of human observation; and
therefore, as it can never present us with indisputable
facts, so must it ever be at once a source of error and
of contention.
If, then, that which we have followed for a true
light, be proved a meteor-if, instead of leading us
into safe and pleasant paths, it have enticed us into
swamps and quagmires-if, instead of informing the
mind, warming and gladdening the heart, it have
clouded and confounded the one, chilled and bruised
the other, are we then without a guide in the path of
life? Are our barks launched upon the ocean, with-
out rudder or compass? To these no star by which
to steer, no rule-directed skill wherewith to trim our
sails, and point an unerring course through the rocks
and whirlpools of our passions and appetites, and the
fogs and deceiving mirage of our deluding and deluded
imaginations. Wo to man, should the answer be a
negative! Wo to

uch a supposition is quite gratuitous, but that is not its least
fault, for the supposition is not sufficient to explain the attendant
phenomena; for if the moon produces such great effects, as she
does, on the ocean, it is because her diurnal angular motion is not
considerable; it is because her distance from the earth scarcely
varies in many hours; it is because the moon corresponds verti-
cally with nearly the same points of the globe during a consider-
able space of time; it is because the fluid which she attracts has
always time to yield to her action, before she moves on to a re-
gion from which her force will operate in another direction. But
this was not the case with the comet of 1680; near the earth its
apparent angular motion across the constellations was extremely
rapid; in a few minutes this comet corresponded with a number
of points on different meridians of the earth placed at a great dis-
tance, the one from the other. As to the distance of the comet from
the earth in a straight line, that, no doubt, may have been very
small, but then this lasted only for a few minutes. These cir
cumstances taken together are very unfavourable for the produc-
tion of a very high tide. I am aware that to meet this difficulty
we have only to enlarge the comet, to make its mass no longer six

produced neither heavenly cataracts, nor internal tides, nor break-
ing up the great abyss; as moreover, neither its tail nor its head
of hair inundated us, we may confidently pronounce the theory of
Whiston to be a mere romance,-unless, abandoning the comet of
1680, we attribute the part it was to play, to another comet much
more considerable.
Let us suppose a solid body moving in a right line with a cer-
tain rapidity, and that an insurmountable obstacle shall be placed
suddenly, all at once on the route of his body, so as to stop it in-
stantly. Let this happen to our earth, the tangential rate of
translation of which is eight leagues in a second, and let this
effect be produced by a comet of sufficient mass, all bodies resting
on its surface, such as animated beings, our car iages, our furni-
ture, our utensils, all objects, in short, which are not fixed in
the ground, would fly towards that point of the earth on which the
comet had struck, with the rapidity which had been communi-
cated to them by the earth's motion-- that is, with a rapidity of
eight leagues per second! One may imagine, at once, what
would be the effect of such an event, when I state that a cannon-
ball of 24lb., even at its first departure from the mouth of the
gun, moves at only 1,200 feet in a second; in a word, every ani-
mated being would be annihilated in one instant!
As to the waters of the ocean, as they are moveable, and as

which have been remarked by geologists, and the effects pro-

But let us suppose by the above, or by any other cometary in-
fluence, vast portions of the continents inundated, and lofty re-
gions buried under water, is it by a violent change such as this
that the marine deposits which have been discovered on moun-
tains have been placed there? These deposits are frequently
horizontal, very extensive, very thick, very regular. The varie-
gated shells, often very small, which compose these layers, often
preserve their projections, their most delicate points, their most
fragile parts. Every thing shows the impossibility of a violent
transportation-everything proves that the deposit has been made
quietly on the spot. What remains, then, to be said to explain
observed geological phenomena, without having recourse to a
violent usurpation of the ocean? We must come to the conclu-
sion that the mountains, as well as the elevated grounds which

s; we have laid bare the bowels of
the earth, disclosed their hidden treasures, and
brought to light the past phenomena of primeval
worlds; we have passed around our globe and ex-
plored its realms and climates through the scorching
tropics to the icy barrier of the poles; we have torn
the lightning from the clouds, and jewels from the
depths of the ocean; we have bowed the elements to
our will, and, appropriating and guiding their strength,
have achieved more than the fabled exploits of demi-
gods, or the miracles of prophets and saints-we have
in truth, in ingenuity, proved ourselves magicians, in
power all but god; yet is our knowledge only igno-
rance, and our wisdom that of babes, seeing that
while exploring the universe we have left unexplored
the human heart, and while mastering the earth we
have still to master ourselves.
Oh! let us not fear, that within the atmosphere of
our own world, in the powers and wants of our own
nature, and in the woes of human life, as originating
in human error, that we may not find a field of inquiry
more than sufficient to fill our time, enchain our
thoughts, and call into action every latent faculty and
feeling of our nature.
Let, then, morals, or the science of human life, as-
sume, among a people boasting themselves free, (and
free, rightly interpreted, would mean rational,) the
place of religion. Let us, instead of speculating and
disputing where we can discover nothing, observe and
inquire where we can discover everything.

1829, by Frances Wright.
THE present is an era of unparalleled interest to the
moral observer, i. e. to him who considers all occur-
rences with a view to their influence on human society.
The principle of change is in all nature, but the
principle of improvement is only (so far as observa-
tion has enabled us to ascertain) in the nature of man.
The scientific eye traces the convulsions of our
earth's solid sphere back, through millions of untold
generations, to eras lost in time, when animals of other
form from those which now move on its surface, ranged
from pole to pole, and (apparently in the absence of
man, whose organic remains seem of more recent ori-
gin) fed on another vegetable kingdom, or preyed on
each other as we now sce their successors. Or let us
observe what is passing around us in the field of exist-
ing nature: Each season brings its vicissitudes, each
passing instant its changes-in the herb, in the flower,
in the forest, in the mountain, in the jewel of the secret
mine; in the vast bed of the ocean-dividing conti-
nents, engulfing or revealing islands, approaching or
receding from its wonted boundaries, until the land-
marks of other days are no more guides to the traveller
or the mariner of these; in all the forms of matter,
whether gaseous, fluid, or solid, whether animate, or,
to our perception, inanimate; in every particle and
unit atom that fills its place, and exercises its agency,
through the endless succession of existence and dura-
tion of time. A

ing its violence, raising a barrier to its accumulated
waters, and bordering its now mastered and innocuous
deluge with the richest productions of human cultiva-
tion. And what we may trace in progress in our own
western regions, we perceive to have taken place
throughout the habited globe. It is man alone, of all
the beings we behold, that hath faculties to distinguish
the alterable phenomena of nature, and power to at-
tempt reform where he distinguishes defect.
Mighty, indeed, are the powers of the human
animal. Through earth, through air, through ocean,
his influence extends. The stamp of his genius is im-
pressed on the whole surface of the globe. Land and sea,
vale and mountain, the howling wilderness of earth's
civilized frontier, the scorched desert of simoom-
swept Africa, the storm-besieged coast and boundless
fields of ocean's restless waters, the glaciers of the
poles, the iced peaks of Alps and towering Andes-
all nature's deep recesses, most stupendous features,
and hidden phenomena, bear witness to his restless
activity, to his dauntless daring, to his aspiring curio-
sity-to his conquering perseverance.
We may be bold to say, that wherever man hath
pierced, and whatever he hath essayed (not absolutely
in contradiction with those unvarying phenomena of
matter to which he has given, albeit inaccurately, the
name of laws,)—wherever he hath been, and whatever
he hath essayed with steady purpose, there, and in
that, he has been

No wings upon quadrupeds, nor fins and scales
on the back of a dog or pigeon. And why does nature
make a fish after one model, a fowl after another
model, and a quadruped or land animal after another
model? Because nature is wise, and what is wisdom?
It is intelligence. And what is intelligence? It is con-
sciousness; and what is consciousness but distinct
and individual existence?
sea.
But some will reply that the laws of nature or
matter, produce all these effects, and in this they
are certainly correct. But the question is, whether
do the laws reside in the body or in the mind, and
what are these laws? To these questions we cannot
reply; all that we can say is, that there are such
things as laws of nature, invariable, unchangeable,
and farther than this we cannot go; and that these
Jaws produce works of infinite variety and wisdom,
and magnificence, by their operations upon matter;
and that so exquisite have been their contrivances and
workmanship hitherto, that we can set no bounds or
limits to their skill and execution in time to come.
These laws are the very excess of grandeur, sublimity,
and skill; in contrivance and execution there is no-
thing so beautiful that they cannot bring it forth,
nothing so ingenious that they cannot invent. But
no man ever saw these laws, or heard them, or felt
them. They are invisible, they are mind; and how
can we prove that they are mind? By the method
and skill whic

text:
one of the candles and its aperture a little farther
off, and you find a black spot where the two rays
meet, showing that the two rays of light uniting toge
ther produce darkness. When, however, the candle
which is shifted comes to a certain distance, the white
spot appears again, and twice that distance it appears
again, and thrice that distance again, and so on for
ever in the same proportion; but if instead of taking
twice the distance, three times, or four times (a
full number), you take one-half the distance or one
half or two and a third, or any fractional part, you pro-
duce a black spot or darkness again. This is called, by
opticians the law of interference, and proves that two
rays of light can neutralize each other, and produce
darkness, which is not less wonderful than that two
species of heat or electric fire uniting together should
produce cold. Indeed, it may be considered as a proof
of the accuracy of this supposition, for there is such a
family likeness throughout all the departments of nature,
that analogy may safely be received as demonstration
where facts cannot be procured.
There is a geographical Trinity which is familiar to
you all, which is created by the inclination of the
axis of the earth to the plane of the ecliptic. This
consists of the three zones of the earth. The torrid
zone, where the sun appears vertical once a year; the
temperate zone, where he declines toward the horizon;
and the frigid zone, where he disappears altogether in
the winter sea
